creator_thesis: @dan_dug_ builds a masculine intellectual identity around history, canon literature, and philosophy, packaging it for Midwest-coded young men who see reading and historical curiosity as a form of cultural rebellion against mediocrity. The account operates at the intersection of "litbro" culture and faith-adjacent traditionalism, using meme formats to lower the barrier to serious ideas. Its underlying argument is that consuming great books and knowing your history is both cool and a distinctly male virtue.

key_concepts.name: Masculine intellectual identity
key_concepts.definition: Reading, history, and philosophy reframed as distinctly male virtues — markers of seriousness and strength rather than nerd signifiers.

key_concepts.name: History-as-meme
key_concepts.definition: Translating crusades, ancient Greece, and civil war figures into relatable, funny formats to make the past feel urgent and personally relevant.

key_concepts.name: Midwest regional identity
key_concepts.definition: Flyover-country pride mythologized as a source of cultural authenticity — 'Middle (West) Earth' — positioned against coastal aesthetic dominance.

key_concepts.name: Anti-slop canon gatekeeping
key_concepts.definition: Active curation of worthy literature (Faulkner, Hemingway, Ellison, Ishiguro, Saunders) as an act of resistance against cultural mediocrity.

key_concepts.name: Christian heritage as living culture
key_concepts.definition: Crusade history, Celtic crosses, Irish high crosses, and Catholic iconography treated as active identity anchors rather than museum artifacts.

key_concepts.name: Historical figures as masculine archetypes
key_concepts.definition: Figures like Teddy Roosevelt, Thucydides, and Ulysses S. Grant presented as aspirational role models embodying virtue, grit, and intellectual seriousness.

key_concepts.name: Propaganda and media criticism
key_concepts.definition: Drawing on Ellul's framework to argue that modern mass media is structurally propagandistic — shaping men's attitudes beyond any single political regime's intent.

references.name: Propaganda: The Formation of Men's Attitudes (Ellul)

positions: Reading has personal, transformative value in the 21st century — best argued through personal history and lived experience rather than abstract cultural prescription.
Propaganda is not a political tool but a structural feature of modern society itself — mass media inherently shapes men's attitudes, per Ellul's framework.
The Midwest is mythologized home terrain ('Middle (West) Earth') — a source of genuine identity that coastal aesthetics can't replicate or co-opt.
'Anti-slop' is a moral stance: choosing Faulkner over filler is an act of seriousness, not elitism — the canon exists to be defended.
Christian and crusade history is not dusty theology but living cultural identity — the Celtic cross's symbolism, the high crosses of Ireland, and the First Crusade are all present-tense inheritance.
